coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified ZnO/CdS nanoparticles based fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe(III)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Nan, Zhezhu
Hao, Changchun
Zhang, Xianggang
Liu, Hengyu
Sun, Runguang
coredata.dc:description:
A simple and fast spectrofluorimetric method coupled with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified ZnO/CdS nanoparticles was developed for the detection of Ferric iron (Fe(III)). The fluorescence of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs was effectively quenched by Fe(III) due to the strong interaction between the CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs and Fe(III). In addition, the detection limit of Fe(III) was about
1.72 ×
10
− 7
M
. The effect of foreign ions on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs showed that the interference response in detecting of Fe(III) ions was low. Moreover, the quenching of Fe(III) and CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs was discussed to be a static quenching procedure, which was proved by quenching constant
K
S V
and fluorescence lifetime τ. The study of thermodynamics showed that the values of entropy change (ΔS) and enthalpy change (ΔH) were both positive, and the value of free energy (ΔG) was negative, which implied that the weak interaction of the molecular between CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs and Fe(III) was hydrophobic force, and the quenching process was endothermic and spontaneous.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ferric iron
Fluorescence sensor
CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs
Static quenching

. (b) Fsample/Fblank (at 427 nm) of the interference metal ions to NPs upon addition of Fe(III) ions. The concentration of interference ions (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, NH4 +, Mn2+, Ag+, Fe2+ and Hg2+) was

and the concentration of Fe(III) ions was

. (c) Deviations from the control sample response caused by the interference ions.

.

Δ H < 0
or

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified ZnO/CdS nanoparticles based fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe(III)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Information Technology, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi'an, 710062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Information Technology, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi'an, 710062, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A simple and fast spectrofluorimetric method coupled with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified ZnO/CdS nanoparticles was developed for the detection of Ferric iron (Fe(III)). The fluorescence of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs was effectively quenched by Fe(III) due to the strong interaction between the CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs and Fe(III). In addition, the detection limit of Fe(III) was about
1.72 ×
10
− 7
M
. The effect of foreign ions on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs showed that the interference response in detecting of Fe(III) ions was low. Moreover, the quenching of Fe(III) and CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs was discussed to be a static quenching procedure, which was proved by quenching constant
K
S V
and fluorescence lifetime τ. The study of thermodynamics showed that the values of entropy change (ΔS) and enthalpy change (ΔH) were both positive, and the value of free energy (ΔG) was negative, which implied that the weak interaction of the molecular between CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs and Fe(III) was hydrophobic force, and the quenching process was endothermic and spontaneous.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (III) ion is ubiquitous in the environment and is an important metal element in living organisms. Iron (III) ions promote the formation of hemoglobin and muscle organisms [1]. However, excessive intake of iron (III) ions can cause aging diseases such as Alzheimer's and other neurogenic diseases, and a lack of these in the body can lead to many diseases, such as anemia and cirrhosis [2–4]. The state of intracellular iron can be reflected by the labile iron pool (LIP) [5]. And the iron has two forms of Fe(II) and Fe(III) in the LIP, when the redox homeostasis of iron is destroyed, it will lead to metabolic disorders, such as oxygen transport and storage, redox, and damage the body's immune mechanism, which cause a variety of diseases [6]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop novel materials to detect iron (III) ions for the treatment of these related diseases.

Methods based on spectrometric techniques such as atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) have been commonly used for detection and quantification of iron (III) ions [7]. However, these techniques not only require expensive and complex equipment, but also generally involve the preparation of complex samples. Fluorescence-based sensing offers significant advantages of high rapidity, sensitivity, selectivity and simplicity, thus, has been extensive applications in environmental monitoring and biomedical [8,9]. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, researchers continue to improve the sensitivity, detection limits, selectivity and reproducibility of sensors, making them better for the detection of pollutants in the environment [10]. Therefore, fluorescence analysis has great potential for development and can be used to detect multiple water pollutants in the field. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were widely used due to excellent properties, such as high quantum yield, biocompatibility, simple synthetic route, high-temperature stability, and low toxicity [11–14]. Previous studies have shown that CQDs contains hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and epoxy groups, which may be functional, organic or polymeric [15]. Semiconducting materials and other smart materials have widely been employed in the technological developments of the state-of-art sensing applications [16,17]. As an important semiconductor material, zinc oxide was widely used in biosensors because of its simple preparation and low toxicity [18]. Also, as a common semiconductor nanoparticle, CdS was often used in sensors [19].

In this study, the nanoparticles were synthesized by simple deposition. The CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs were characterized by X-ray diffractograms (XRD), field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM), UV–visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) techniques. Moreover, it can be used to investigated the selective and sensitive detection of Fe(III), and the influence of interference ions. Finally, the detection limit of Fe(III) ions and the quenching mechanism by CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs were discussed.

Zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2), Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH), sodium sulfide (Na2S), Copper chloride (CuCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), nickel chloride (NiCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), iron(II) chloride (FeCl2), silver nitrate (AgNO3), mercury(II) nitrate (Hg(NO3)2) and iron(III) chloride (FeCl3) were purchased from Tianli Chemicla Reagent Co. Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade without further purification and all solutions were prepared in double distilled water.

First, ZnO nanoparticles were prepared using zinc acetate and sodium hydroxide [20]. 1 M NaOH aqueous solution was added drop-wise to 1 M Zn(CH3COO)2 aqueous solution. After stirring for half an hour, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed alternately three times with ethanol and distilled water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 2 h.

5 μM Cd(NO3)2 aqueous solution and 5 μM Na2S aqueous solution were added drop-wise in 0.5 M Zn(CH3COO)2 aqueous solution, and then 0.5 M NaOH aqueous solution was added. After stirring for half an hour, the obtained precipitate was subjected to the same operation as that for preparing ZnO, centrifuged, stirred, and dried to obtain CdS/ZnO composite nanoparticles.

A facile one-step process obtained CQDs [21]. 5 mmol citric acid was poured into 10 mL water. The solution was flowed into 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h, then cooled down to room temperature. Finally, the CQDs solution was prepared by dialysis for 24 h.

The preparation process of CQDs/ZnO/CdS composites was carried out as follows: 0.5 g CdS/ZnO powder was dispersed in 30 mL of distilled water, followed by a stirring continued for 30 min, and then the CQDs solution was added into the as prepared CdS/ZnO mixture. After being stirred for 12 h, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed alternately three times with ethanol and distilled water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 12 h. Finally, the samples were heated at 250 °C in a muffle furnace for 1 h. Thus, CQDs/ZnO/CdS composites nanoparticles were prepared.

Photon Technology International (PTI) QM/TM-40 spectrophotometer equipped with a thermostatic bath and a 75W Xenon lamp was used to measure fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, using the 5 nm slit widths. The crystal structure of the prepared nanoparticles was judged by XRD (X-ray diffractometry, Rigaku, Japan). The morphology and structure of ZnO, ZnO/CdS and CQDs/ZnO/CdS were recorded on a field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova NanoSEM 450, FEI) after dripping onto silicon wafer. The UV–visible absorption spectra were confirmed using a spectrophotometer UV1901PC (Shanghai AuCy Technology Instrument Co., LTD).

3.00 mL of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs dispersion solution (
1 ×
10
− 5
M
) was added the various doses of Fe(III) solution ranges from 0.5 mL to 5 mL into the 10 mL test tubes. The selectivity experiments were performed using 3.00 mL of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs dispersion solution containing
1 ×
10
− 5
M
Fe(III) and
5 ×
10
− 5
M
interference ions. The interference ions selected for this work were Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, NH4 +, Mn2+, Ag+, Fe2+ and Hg2+.

Fig. 1 (a) showed XRD spectrums of nanoparticles (ZnO, ZnO/CdS and CQDs/ZnO/CdS, respectively), the characteristic diffraction peaks at (2θ) 30.41°, 33.87°, 36.36°, 47.92°, 55.59°, 63.46°and 68.64° could be index to the typical (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) crystal plane of ZnO, respectively [22]. The XRD pattern of the prepared CdS showed various well-defined diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26.74°, 44.09°, and 52.37° analogous to the (111), (220) and (311) planes of the hexagonal structure of CdS. The characteristic peaks of the sample were well match to the ZnO and CdS, which confirmed the formation of ZnO and CdS. In the peak of CdS, low intensity and broad peaks were appeared, which demonstrated that the size of the CdS nanoparticles was relatively small [23]. No typical peaks of CQDs were found in the XRD pattern of CQDs/ZnO/CdS materials, which could be ascribed to the low amount of CQDs in the nanocomposite [24].

In UV–Vis absorption spectrums of nanoparticles (ZnO, ZnO/CdS and CQDs/ZnO/CdS, respectively) were displayed in Fig. 1(b). The spectra of pure ZnO possessed an absorption at around 370 nm corresponding to the band gap of ZnO similar to that was observed in previous reports [25,26]. The characteristic absorption peak of CdS existed in the absorption spectrum of ZnO/CdS at 554 nm. And the disappearance of the sharp peak of ZnO in ZnO/CdS nanocomposites was due to the (O2p → Zn3d) electron transitions from the valence band to the conduction band, which result in the consequence that the characteristic peak of ZnO may be allocated to the intrinsic band gap absorption of ZnO [25,27,28]. The absorption spectra of CQDs/ZnO/CdS nanocomposites were similar to that of ZnO/CdS nanoparticles. Also the CQDs/ZnO/CdS nanocomposites had a high value of absorbance in the visible region compared to pristine ZnO/CdS nanoparticles.

Fig. 2 (a) showed the SEM image of pure ZnO, which was clearly observed the aggregation of nanoparticles. Fig. 2(b) and (c) illustrated the SEM images of ZnO/CdS and CQDs/ZnO/CdS, respectively. The morphology of ZnO/CdS nanoparticles demonstrated the addition of CdS had an effect on the aggregation of ZnO, leading to the decrease of the nanoparticles size finally. From Fig. 2(c), CQDs were successfully doped to the ZnO/CdS nanoparticles, resulting from the increase of the nanoparticles size.

According to Fig. 2(d), the prepared nanoparticles had a maximum excitation peak at a wavelength of 340 nm. In the range of emission wavelength of 410–610 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was obtained by exciting nanoparticles at the maximum excitation peak of 427 nm.The maximum excitation peak and maximum emission peak of the nanoparticles were consistent with those reported in the literature, indicating that CQDs was successfully attached to the surface of the nanoparticles [29].

It is well know that the study of sensors stability is very important. The effects of temperature and pH on fluorescence stability of nanoparticles were studied. Fig. 2(e) and (f) revealed the influences of pH and temperature on fluorescence spectra, respectively. Fig. 2(e) indecated that the fluorescence intensity of nanoparticles had no obvious change with the increase of pH. The fluorescence value was the largest at pH = 7, which indicated that pH had little effect on nanoparticles Also, it was suitable for measuring metal ions at low pH. Fig. 2(f) showed the relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature. It confirmed that there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity when the temperature was below room temperature. In addition, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increase of temperature, which indicated that the stability of nanoparticles decreased with the increase of temperature [30].

The detection of metal ions by fluorescence quenching was one of the important means of fluorescence detection. The interaction between metal ions and fluorophores caused the fluorophore to be quenched. Real samples often involved complex matrices that may interfere with the accurate detection of the target analyst. As such, the applicability of analytical methods was highly dependent on its selectivity. Therefore, the selectivity of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs was evaluated using typical aquatic interfering cations.

Fig. 3 (a) showed the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs in the presence of physiologically significant metal ions. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. The results revealed the fluorescence intensity of NPs was strongly quenched by Fe(III). As for the effect of other cations (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, NH3 +, Mn2+, Ag+, Fe2+ and Hg2+) on the fluorescence intensity of NPs was weak. The values of F/F0 is 0.15 corresponding to the ions being Fe(III), and the values of F/F0 for other ions are between 0.96 and 1.07. From the above results, it was speculated that NPs had a potential for specific recognition of Fe(III) ions, and it was necessary to add interference ions to detect the specific recognition ability of NPs for Fe(III) in the presence of interference ions. The quenching effect of Fe(III) was investigated by comparing the F/F0 ratios in the presence of different interference ions. In the absence and presence of
5 ×
10
− 5
M
interference ions, the measured F/F0 ratios quench by
1 ×
10
− 5
M
Fe(III) was showed in Fig. 3(b). In the figure, Fblank was the fluorescence intensity of adding
1 ×
10
− 5
M
Fe(III) into the NPs solution, and Fsample was the fluorescence intensity of the NPs solution contacting
5 ×
10
− 5
M
interference ions and
1 ×
10
− 5
M
Fe(III). It was found that the values of Fsample/Fblank were between 0.96 and 1.19, the results indicated that these ions did not have significant interference on CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs as Fe(III) irons probes. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for three replicate determinations of Fe(III) ions was estimated as 3.13%, and the FL intensity measurement errors caused by all interference ions investigated were within a range of 1.8% and 3.4%(Fig. 3(c)). This results proved the CQD/ZnO/CdS NPs could be used as selective Fe(III) ions probes.

Such a unique quenching characteristic of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs to Fe(III), conduced to construction of sensing applications. The relationship between fluorescence intensities and Fe(III) ions concentration was then established. Fig. 4 (a) showed the fluorescence spectra of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs adding into Fe(III) ions with the concentration range from 0.1 mL to 1 mL. It could be shown from the Figure that the trend was a decrease in fluorescence intensity as the Fe(III) irons concentration increase. Fig. 4(b) showed the plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency (
F Q E = (
F
0
− F ) /
F
0
)
) against Fe(III) concentration. A liner relationship can be determined within a concentration range of
0 − 5 ×
10
− 5
M
, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency fitted the linear equation:
Y = 1.34 ×
10
5
X − 0.00248
(
R
2
= 0.993
).The limit of detection (LOD) could be estimated to be
1.72 ×
10
− 7
M
by the detection limit calculation formula (Eq. (1)) [9].The analytical performances could determine the feasibility of NPs as the Fe(III) irons probes.
(1)
LOD = 3 σ / κ
Where
κ
is slope of lines,
σ
is standard error.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cyanide in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of CQDs/ZnO/CdS NPs based on sensing method for determination of Fe(III) was evaluated using tap water samples,which were collected from various sources (Table 1 ). From Table 1, the recoveries were found to be 98.8–103.8%. This result indicated that the present sensor could be applied to detection of Fe(III) ions in real water.

In addition, it was found that the sensor reported in this paper has no particularly low accuracy detection compared with other literatures (Table 2 ), but the detection range was relatively wide, which could also be used for the detection of Fe(III) in real water.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism mainly included static and dynamic quenching, as well as combine quenching of dynamic and static. In general, Eq. (2) can determine the type of fluorescence quenching mechanism [36].
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[
Q
]
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of absence and presence Fe(III) ions in the NPs solution, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of Fe(III) ions.
K
S V
is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

K
S V
is an important parameter to judge the type of fluorescence quenching. In the dynamic quenching process,
K
S V
decreases with the increase of temperature, while in the static quenching process, it is opposite. Dynamic quenching is a process in which the fluorescence intensity is reduced by the collision of quencher and fluorophore. The dynamic quenching mechanism is the loss of the excited state energy caused by the collision, which makes the excited state return to the ground state. Since the increase of temperature will cause the increasing of the diffusion coefficient, the
K
S V
of dynamic quenching increases with the increasing of temperature. Static quenching is a process in which non-fluorescence or weak fluorescence compounds are formed between quencher and fluorophore to reduce the fluorescence intensity. The KSV of static quenching decreases with the increasing of temperature, because the increasing of temperature reduces the stability of complex [37]. Therefore, it is essential to explore the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe(III) ions in the NPs solution at different temperatures (298K, 303K, 313K). The linear Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F against [Fe(III)] at different temperatures (298 K, 306 K and 313 K) was shown in Fig. 5 (a). The values of
K
S V
could be obtained by Eq (2), which was recorded in Table 1. It was observed from Table 1 that the values of
K
S V
decreases with temperature, which was indicated that the quenching mechanism was static quenching between Fe(III) and NPs.

The fluorescence lifetime could also be used for the analysis of quenching principle. From Fig. 5(b), it was observed that Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F against [Fe(III)] at room temperature and time resolved fluorescence quenching of CQDs/ZnO/CdS nanoparticles by Fe(III) ions. The non-linear relationship between F0 and [Fe(III)] indicated that the quenching process was static quenching, and the linear relationship between relative fluorescence lifetime (
τ
0
/ τ
) and concentration was almost parallel to the X-axis, which further indicated that the process was static quenching. The reason is that the process of static quenching will not result in the decrease of fluorescence lifetime [38].

For a static quenching process, the number of binding constant (K
A ) and binding sites (n) between NPs and Fe(III) ions could be determined by using the double logarithm regression curve (Eq. (3)) [39].
(3)
log
F
0
− F
F
= n l og
K
A
− n l og
(
1
[
Q
]
−
(
F
0
− F
)
[
P
]
F
0
)
Where n is the number of binding sites, KA is the binding constant, [P] is the concentration of NPs, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of absence and presence Fe(III) ions in the NPs solution, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of Fe(III) ions.

The Fig. 5(c) showed the plot of log((F0 − F)/F) against log(1/([Fe(III)] − [NPs](F0 − F)/F0). The values of KA and n were obtained from the Y-axis and the linear slope, respectively (Table 3 ). According to Eq. (3), as the temperature increases, the KA value decreased, indicating that the degree of binding of NPs and Fe(III) decreased with the increasing temperature. The quenching mechanism of NPs and Fe(III) ions was mainly determined by static quenching, which further confirmed this. It can be seen from the previous literatures that the higher the binding ability of the fluorophore and the quencher are, the higher the KA is, which usually greater than 104 L/mol, and lower than 105 L/mol for KA indicates the opposite [40]. Therefore, the KA value between NPs and Fe(III) indicates poor binding ability. For the NPs-Fe(III) system, values of n = 1.184, 1.124, and 1.043 at 298 K, 306 K, and 313 K, respectively, confirmed the presence of a single binding site in the NPs during interaction with Fe(III) [41].

The main driving forces involved in the interaction between drugs and biomacromolecules are hydrophobic action, hydrogen bond, electrostatic attraction and van der Waals force [42]. In research on the basis of thermodynamics in the enthalpy change (Δ H) and entropy (Δ S) parameters to determine the main forces between compounds and proteins. Table 4 shows the relationship between enthalpy and entropy changes and their corresponding interactions. Therefore, equations of Van't Hoff (Eq. (4)) and Gibbs - Helmholtz (Eq. (5)) were used to calculate the enthalpy change (Δ H), entropy change (Δ S) and free energy (Δ G) [43].
(4)
log
K
A
= −
Δ H
2.303 R T
+
Δ S
2.303 R
(5)
Δ G = Δ H − T Δ S
where ΔH and ΔS are the change of enthalpy and entropy, respectively; R is the gas constant; ΔG is the Gibbs free energy.

The diagram of logKA against 1/T was shown in Fig. 6 . ΔH and ΔS can be obtained from the slope and intercept in the Fig. 6, then ΔG under different temperature were calculated respectively with the Eq. (5). And the values of ΔH, ΔS and ΔG were recorded in Table 3. As can be seen from the data in the Table 4, ΔH and ΔS are positive values, so the interaction between Fe(III) and NPs is judged as hydrophobic. In addition, the Gibbs free energy (ΔG) is negative. And the higher the temperature is, the greater the absolute value of ΔG is, indicating that the interaction between Fe(III) and NPs is spontaneous and the higher the temperature is, the more likely the interaction will occur. To sum up, the interaction between Fe(III) and NPs is hydrophobic interaction, and the process is spontaneous with endothermic and entropy increase [42].

CQD/ZnO/CdS NPs were successfully prepared and used as a highly sensitive and fast Fe(III) ions fluorescent nanosensor. The results showed that the foreign ions don't have significantly interfere with the detection of Fe(III) ions, and the detection limit was calculated to be
1.72 ×
10
− 7
M
. In addition, the detection range is within 1–50 μM, making the CQD/ZnO/CdS nanosensor more accurate. The quenching mechanism between the composite nanoparticles and Fe(III) ions should be static quenching. Through thermodynamic analysis, the interaction of nanoparticles and Fe(III) ions may be the strong hydrophobic forces.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.
We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11874039), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (GK201803021 and 2018CSLY004).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The essential metals for humans: a brief overview
J. Inorg. Biochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Zoroddu, J. Aaseth, G. Crisponi, S. Medici, M. Peana, V.M. Nurchi, The essential metals for humans: a brief overview, J. Inorg. Biochem. 195 2019 (120-129).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An attempt to elucidate the role of iron and zinc ions in development of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases
Biomed. Pharmacother.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.K. Karolina, O. Anna, W.H. Monika, An attempt to elucidate the role of iron and zinc ions in development of Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases, Biomed Pharmacother. 111 (2019) 1277-1289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The molecular mechanisms of the metabolism and transport of iron innormal and neoplastic cells
Biochim. Biophys. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.R. Richardson, P. Ponka, The molecular mechanisms of the metabolism and transport of iron innormal and neoplastic cells, Biochim. Biophys. Acta. 1331 (1997) 1-40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent probes for the detection of catalytic Fe(II) ion
Free Radical Biol. Med.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Hirayama, Fluorescent probes for the detection of catalytic Fe(II) ion, Free Radical Biol. Med. 133 (2019) 38-45.
